title: Parameters in the Output Sequence
text: Parameters in the Output Sequence Some mnemonics include parameters in the output sequence that can be used to determine layout and formatting information, such as horizontal and vertical coordinates, numeric base zero (decimal, octal, or hexadecimal), zero padding, and so on. The output sequence can even include expressions that use the parameters incalculation, for example, to increment values. In many cases, Uniface adapts the way it operates based on which parameters are included. The exact parameters passed and how Uniface reacts are described with each mnemonic. For example, if the %p parameter is not included in the output sequence for the buzz mnemonic, Uniface assumes that the device cannot buzz several times with one command; the buzz command is then activated p times. Table 1. Parameters in the output sequence Parameters Meaning %h %v Horizontal and vertical starting coordinates. %m Repetition ( m times) of character. Useful for white space. %m %n Horizontal width and vertical depth. %x %y Horizontal and vertical ending coordinates. %p %q General parameters, often used as a kind of counter. %w %d Character width and depth, that is, resolution. Formatting the Output Sequence All of these parameters can be preceded by a number which determines how Uniface formats the data, that is, which base (decimal, octal, or hexadecimal), and whether the number should be padded with zeros to a fixed length. The syntax is shown in the table, where a question mark (?) is a single digit 1 through 9: Table 2. Data Formatting Syntax Parameters Meaning %0p or %p Decimal value with variable number of digits (that is, no padding). %?p Decimal value padded with leading zeros to ? digits. %10p Octal value with variable number of digits. %1?p Octal value padded with leading zeros to ? digits. %20p Hexadecimal value with variable number of digits. %2?p Hexadecimal value padded with leading zeros to ? digits. %30p Binary value, bytes ordered low to high, variable number of bytes. %3?p Binary value, bytes ordered low to high. %40p Binary value, bytes ordered high to low, variable number of bytes. %4?p Binary value, bytes ordered high to low. For example, if Uniface needs to pass the value 17 as a parameter, the table shows the literal values that are generated with the corresponding codes: Table 3. Examples: Data Formatting Parameters Parameter Meaning Value generated %0p Decimal. 17 %3p Decimal, padded to three digits. 017 %14p Octal, padded to four digits. 0021 %20p Hexadecimal. 11 Expressions in an Output Sequence Uniface also supports expressions within an output sequence. An expression must be enclosed in parentheses. The indicator for base and padding of the final result goes between the percent sign (%) and the opening bracket (() of the expression. Values within the expression are always decimal; formatting occurs at the last step. All operators supported by the ProcScript language are available here, including brackets (()). The expressions can use integer constants and all parameters appropriate for the particular mnemonic. Parameters within the expression are not preceded by a percent sign (%). Spaces are allowed within the expressions to increase readability. If a part of the expression contains a comparison operator (for example, =, <, >, and so on) that section expression is evaluated as a Boolean expression. For example, %(2+2<(2+1)) is evaluated as 3. A few examples are given in the table: Table 4. Examples: Expressions in Parameter Declarations Parameters Meaning %(p+1) Increase ‘p’ by one. %24(8 * (y + w * 16)) Use ‘y’ and ‘w’ in a calculation. %(p%8) Use modulus to compute a value between 0 and 7, inclusive.
